meta_description: PatchPlane keeps AI coding requests, runs, reviews, and merge decisions in one place so teams can ship changes without losing context or control.
header_tagline: Control AI coding work from request to merge
footer_description: See every AI coding request, run, review, and decision in one place so your team can ship changes with control.
landing_badge: For teams shipping with coding agents
landing_title: Stop losing AI-generated changes across prompts, runs, and reviews.
landing_lede: PatchPlane keeps every request, agent run, review, and merge decision in one control plane so your team can move faster without losing context, ownership, or approval history.
landing_facts_shared_surface_detail: AI change work gets scattered across chats, terminals, CI, and pull requests.
landing_facts_single_thread_detail: PatchPlane keeps the request, run, review, and decision in one operational thread.
landing_facts_operator_readable_detail: Your team can review and ship AI-generated changes without losing context or control.
landing_signal_intro_body: Instead of reconstructing what happened from four different tools, operators get one live thread from request to merge decision.
landing_signal_1_detail: record the repo, scope, policy, and owner before work begins
landing_signal_2_detail: stream tool calls, artifacts, and failures into one readable timeline
landing_signal_3_detail: keep checks, pass or fail signals, and merge readiness attached to the same change
landing_signal_footer: One place to see the work, review it, and decide what happens next.
landing_workflow_title: PatchPlane solves the handoff problem in three steps.
landing_workflow_intro: The pain is not just generating code. The pain is tracking what was requested, what happened during the run, and whether the result is safe to ship.
landing_workflow_1_title: AI work gets fragmented the moment it leaves the prompt.
landing_workflow_1_summary: Teams lose time jumping between chat transcripts, terminal output, CI logs, and PR comments to reconstruct what changed.
landing_workflow_2_title: PatchPlane turns that sprawl into one shared control plane.
landing_workflow_2_summary: Every request, run, artifact, review, and decision stays linked in the same operational surface.
landing_workflow_3_title: You can approve and ship AI-generated changes with confidence.
landing_workflow_3_summary: Operators keep provenance, interrupt paths, and decision history even when multiple agents are working at once.
landing_proof_title: Everything needed to move from request to merge.
landing_proof_intro: These are the product mechanics that matter because they answer the buyer questions immediately: what problem is solved, how it is solved, and what happens next.
landing_proof_1_summary: Start with a scoped change request instead of an untracked prompt.
landing_proof_2_summary: See runtime events and artifacts without opening a stack of tool-specific consoles.
landing_proof_3_summary: Keep checks, comments, and pass or fail signals beside the work itself.
landing_proof_4_summary: Know what shipped, why it passed, and how to trace every decision back to the original request.
landing_platform_title: Built for teams that want speed without blind spots.
landing_platform_intro: PatchPlane is not another chat window. It is the operational layer between agent output and a merge decision, so teams can move faster without giving up oversight.
landing_story_signal_shared_surface: Shared surface for operators and reviewers
landing_story_signal_runtime_boundaries: Clear runtime, policy, and approval boundaries
landing_cta_title: Open the app and see the full request-to-review flow.
landing_cta_body: If you want to use PatchPlane, start in the browser app. It shows the product immediately, and the architecture notes are there when you want the deeper implementation view.
about_hero_title: Keep the workspace small until the execution loop is proven.
about_hero_lede: PatchPlane should earn its package boundaries by proving one real flow: request intake, runtime activity, review output, and a visible decision trail.
about_principles_title: Architecture notes belong here, not on the landing page.
about_principles_intro: These principles explain why the current workspace stays compact and where future extraction pressure should come from.
about_principle_1_detail: One TanStack Start app owns both the landing page and the browser product shell so the product language stays consistent while the loop is still forming.
about_principle_2_detail: `packages/backend` keeps Convex, runtime contracts, and sandbox boundaries together until the first execution path is real enough to justify extraction.
about_principle_3_detail: Runtime adapters, policy packages, and desktop-specific bridges should split only after request coordination, runtime normalization, and review orchestration are exercised end to end.
about_package_map_title: Each package has one clear job today.
about_package_map_intro: That keeps the codebase small enough to move quickly without hiding runtime decisions in too many layers.
about_package_1_role: Landing page, browser product shell, and shared visual language.
about_package_2_role: Convex backend root plus runtime, sandbox, and policy boundaries.
about_package_3_role: Shared types, schemas, workflow statuses, and capability language.
about_bootstrap_title: Local commands and near-term implementation work stay operational.
about_bootstrap_intro: This is the place for setup notes, current constraints, and the next slice of work that should turn the shell into a real control plane.
about_next_slice_body: Wire one request mutation, one fake run action, and one event feed from Convex before introducing more runtimes, shells, or packaging layers.
app_hero_intro: This route is the working surface for coordinated change requests. Keep it operational, thin, and ready to survive both browser and desktop shells.
app_auth_intro: Convex auth state now gates the operational surface, and the browser can use authenticated queries once the backend validates the WorkOS token.
app_sign_in_prompt: Sign in to access authenticated Convex data.
app_auth_loading: Checking Convex authentication and exchanging the WorkOS token.
app_unauthenticated: The command center stays readable while signed out, but protected data stays behind Convex auth.
app_status_queued_detail: Request is recorded, scoped, and waiting for execution.
app_status_running_detail: Tool calls and artifacts are arriving in the shared event feed.
app_status_reviewed_detail: Review output is attached and ready to inform the decision.
app_workflow_status_detail: Each status belongs in the shared domain package and should receive Convex-backed transitions before new product chrome is added.
app_focus_intro: Wire one request mutation, one fake run action, and one event feed before introducing heavier integrations. Product credibility comes from a readable loop, not from more shells.
app_panel_1_detail: Prompt intake, repo scope, and policy context should all resolve into one request record before execution begins.
app_panel_2_detail: Normalized events should make tool calls, artifacts, and failures readable without opening runtime-specific consoles.
app_panel_3_detail: Review output should sit beside the run so the operator can make merge or rollback calls from the same surface.
app_authenticated_welcome: Welcome {name}. Convex validated your WorkOS token before loading this data.
